alleged_violation_details.workforce_member_or_department_name: Customer Service / Claims Department – Representative identified as "Karen" (would not provide last name)
alleged_violation_details.describe_the_privacy_violation:
I am filing this complaint because I believe my HIPAA privacy rights were violated during and after a phone call with HWMG’s customer service/claims department on 2025-11-18.

Background: I receive behavioral health counseling once a week for anxiety and depression related to a difficult divorce. I work full-time as an office manager at a small architecture firm. My employer offers group health coverage through HWMG, and my coverage is under that plan. Because of the stigma around mental health at my workplace, I have been very careful about who knows I am in therapy. I specifically do not want my employer or my direct supervisor to know the details of my treatment.

On 2025-11-18, around 10:15 a.m. Pacific Time, I called HWMG customer service from my desk phone at work to ask about a denied claim for a therapy session that occurred on 2025-10-30. I was connected to a representative who identified herself only as "Karen" in the claims department. I explained that I was at work, in an open office environment, and asked if we could keep the discussion general and avoid mentioning specific diagnoses or sensitive details out loud because my coworkers sit very close to me.

Despite my request, the representative immediately began reading out loud specific diagnostic codes and descriptions associated with my behavioral health claims. She stated, in a normal speaking voice (not lowered), that the denial was connected to "Major Depressive Disorder, recurrent episode, moderate" and "Generalized Anxiety Disorder," and then read several dates of service and provider names, including my therapist’s name and the phrase "psychotherapy, 60 minutes with patient." At least two of my coworkers, including my assistant, were at their desks within a few feet of me and could clearly hear her side of the conversation and, based on their later comments to me, parts of mine.

I tried to interrupt and told her again, very clearly, "I’m at work in a shared space, please don’t read my diagnoses out loud, just tell me generally what the problem with the claim is and I can log into the portal later." She responded that she was "required" to read the full information and continued to read line items from my Explanation of Benefits, including specific mental health procedure codes and the words "behavioral health" and "depression treatment." I was extremely embarrassed because one of my coworkers later asked me if I was "okay" and said she "didn’t know it was that serious," clearly indicating she had overheard at least some of the details.

In addition, during this same call, I told the representative that I did NOT want any further written communications about my mental health treatment sent to my employer’s office address, and asked if we could update my mailing preferences so that all future correspondence related to behavioral health claims would be sent to my home address only or made available solely through the secure online portal. She told me that "all plan-related mail has to go to the employer’s address" and that there was no way to separate behavioral health information or send it to my home. I asked specifically for "confidential communications" and for restrictions on how my information is disclosed, and she flatly refused, saying that "the system doesn’t allow that" and that "if you don’t want your employer to see it, you can just not use the insurance for those visits."

A few days later, on 2025-11-22, an Explanation of Benefits (EOB) arrived at my employer’s office addressed to me but placed openly in the shared mail bin. The EOB clearly listed the name of my therapist, the service description ("psychotherapy, 60 minutes"), and the diagnosis codes. My office manager (who distributes mail) saw it and handed it to me in front of my coworkers, commenting, "Looks like something from your insurance about counseling." This further confirmed my fear that my mental health information is being disclosed in a way that is not reasonably confidential.

I believe the following rights were violated:
1. Breach of confidentiality: My protected health information (specifically, mental health diagnoses and treatment details) was disclosed verbally in a setting that the representative knew was not private, after I explicitly asked her not to read that information out loud.
2. Denial of confidential communications: I specifically requested that communications about my mental health treatment be directed to my home address or handled through secure electronic means, and I was told this was not possible and was refused any accommodation.
3. Denial of request to restrict use/disclosure: I requested a restriction on how my protected health information is disclosed (particularly to my employer’s office address), and that request was dismissed without any attempt to document or formally process it.

This situation has caused me significant distress and anxiety. I feel that my very personal mental health information has been exposed in my workplace, where it could affect how my supervisor and coworkers view me and potentially impact my job security and opportunities for advancement.
alleged_violation_details.what_action_if_any_do_you_believe_will_c:
I am asking HWMG to take several corrective actions to address what happened and to prevent similar violations in the future.

1. Immediate changes to my communication preferences and restrictions:
   - Honor my request for confidential communications by updating my account so that all future communications related to behavioral health or other sensitive services (including Explanations of Benefits, denial notices, appeals decisions, and any correspondence that contains diagnostic or treatment information) are sent only to my home mailing address:
     1843 Willow Bend Drive, Portland, OR 97219.
   - Where possible, provide these documents through the secure online member portal instead of paper mail, and send only generic, non-detailed notices by mail (for example, "You have a new document in your portal" without specifying that it is related to mental health or behavioral health).
   - Document in my file that I have requested a restriction on disclosures of my behavioral health information to my employer’s address and to any third parties not directly involved in my care or payment.

2. Written acknowledgment and explanation:
   - Provide me with a written explanation of how this incident is being investigated, including whether the call recording has been reviewed and whether it confirms that sensitive information was read aloud after I requested discretion.
   - Provide a written acknowledgment that my request for confidential communications and restrictions on disclosure has been received, processed, and either accepted or, if any part is denied, a clear explanation of the specific legal or operational reason for the denial.

3. Staff training and policy review:
   - Require additional training for the customer service/claims representative involved in this call ("Karen") and, ideally, for the entire customer service staff, focusing specifically on:
     * How to handle calls when a member states they are in a non-private environment (such as work) and requests discretion.
     * The member’s right to request confidential communications and restrictions on certain uses and disclosures of protected health information.
     * The heightened sensitivity of mental and behavioral health information and the need to minimize unnecessary verbal disclosure of diagnoses and details over the phone.
   - Review and, if necessary, update internal scripts and procedures so that representatives are not required to read full diagnostic descriptions aloud if a member has requested that they not do so, especially when the member indicates they are at work or in a shared space.

4. Process improvement regarding mail to employer addresses:
   - Review HWMG’s policy that all plan-related mail must go to the employer’s address. If this is simply a default practice and not a legal requirement, I ask that you change it to allow members to designate a home address for all communications that contain PHI, particularly mental health information.
   - If there is a legitimate reason some documents must go to the employer’s address, develop a process to ensure those documents are as generic as possible and do not list specific diagnoses or detailed treatment descriptions.

5. Assurance of non-retaliation:
   - Provide written assurance that filing this complaint will not result in any retaliation against me as a member, including denial of coverage, delays in processing claims, or any negative impact on my benefits.

6. Optional: Notification to my employer only if required by law (and without specific details):
   - I am not requesting that my employer be informed of the specifics of this complaint. However, if HWMG determines that some notification or clarification to my employer is necessary (for example, to change how mail is handled), I request that any communication be limited to operational details (such as changing my mailing address in the system) and NOT include any information about my diagnoses, therapy, or the content of this complaint.

Overall, what I want most is to regain a sense of privacy and control over my health information. I want to be able to continue using my behavioral health benefits without fear that my employer or coworkers will inadvertently learn details about my treatment. I believe the actions above would help correct the immediate problem and reduce the likelihood that another member experiences the same kind of embarrassment and anxiety that I did.
